author: @marcfruchtman9473
text: I think you are incredibly creative... humans are horrible at learning in a vacuum. So, we stand on the shoulder's of everyone who has come before us to learn and progress. I really appreciate that you spend the time to review prior works and display your vision of it. Thank you for doing what you do.

author: @Happy.Chappy
text:
You're 100% right. 

When dealing with negativity and unpleasantness anywhere in life, it's important to remember that happy, fullfilled, well adjusted people do not act this way. 

I have also learned to pity them. Even empathise. The world is a mirror they say... Everything you do is a reflection of self...

All the best mate, one of my fav YouTubers!

text: So much wisdom. I wonder if Luke knows how fortunate he is to have a dad like you.

author: @stuartbarker9373
text: Occasionally I've clicked the dislike button when I meant to click the like one. Probably not on any of your channels, but I can't be sure. It usually happens when I'm clicking when the vid is almost over or has just started, when the position of these buttons can shift. On the assumption that others are similarly caught out by youtube's frustrating design, it's probably best not to read too much into the number of dislikes.

author: @HarleyPebley
text: Re: copying: in science, reproducing results is part and parcel of validating conclusions. Copying is a fundamental part of the scientific method.

author: @mightystudiosvids
text: Thanks for this. Very helpful advice. We're all doing riffs on ideas someone else sparked, including ideas you've shared yourself. My only complaint is you get me super interested in a topic, and while I'm learning and tinkering with it you put out a new video on a different, equally-interesting topic. By the time my 3D print is done you're already two topics ahead! I can't keep this pace. :)

author: @timmydeee
text: As always, a nicely presented and we'll rounded breakdown of your thoughts. Ironically, the next video in my feed is from William Osman!

author: @totherarf
text:
Firstly Happy Christmas to each and every single one of you!
Secondly .... I think that some of the comments may just be misunderstood. I think the root may be the media used. Often I comment and read it back before posting and have to change it because the translation from my inner voice to the printed word can loose nuance and even meaning!
Maybe if enough people recognise this we will create a system of glyphs that show intent behind the words, ....... but maybe not ;o)

author: @pavanbiliyar
text:
I try to remember the famous Eleanor Roosevelt quote when dealing with people, 

"Great minds talk about ideas,
Mediocre minds talk about events,
and Weak minds talk about people."

That being said, I recently discovered that the Dunning-Kruger effect isn't limited to the stereotypically unintelligent; it affects us all.  Simply pull us out of our comfort zone and see how we react.  Humble folks will be patient and open to learning new things, expanding knowledge. 

But then you have the impatient folks who only believe their passions have substance and everything else has none, so they will make assumptions and jump to conclusions thinking there's nothing to it.  

The Dunning-Kruger effect is about the overconfidence people have from learning so little, like they heard enough or its no big deal.  You don't have to be dumb to suffer from the Dunning-Kruger effect.
